---
output:
  xaringan::moon_reader:
    css: ["default", "extra.css"]
    lib_dir: libs
    seal: false
    nature:
      highlightStyle: github
      highlightLines: true
      countIncrementalSlides: false
      ratio: '16:9'
---

```{r, echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE}
##xaringan::inf_mr()
## For offline work: https://bookdown.org/yihui/rmarkdown/some-tips.html#working-offline
## Images not appearing? Put images folder inside the libs folder as that is the main data directory

library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(stargazer)
##library(kableExtra)
##library(modelr)

knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = FALSE,
                      eval = TRUE,
                      error = FALSE,
                      message = FALSE,
                      warning = FALSE,
                      comment = NA)
```

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-worldmap3.png')
background-size: 105%
background-class: center
class: middle

.size45[**I. Arguments, Evidence and International Relations**]

.size45[

**Today's Agenda**

1. What is "science"?

2. What is "politics"?

3. Can it be studied "scientifically"?
]

<br>

.center[.size40[
  Justin Leinaweaver (Spring 2024)
]]

???

### Prep for Class
1. Review and record participation submissions

2. Publish next discussion post

3. Print "get out of jail free" cards for our distribution game (print from GDrive)

4. Update with class notes from trying to define our concepts
    - International: Involves the actions of more than one country
    - Political: *we didn't really get to this*
    - Event: A thing that happened

<br>

Welcome back everybody!

On Monday we collected case studies in order to define an international political event.

### What did we come up with? Define an international political event for me.

### - Examples that meet the definition?

<br>

Today we continue laying important foundations for the rest of the semester.

- We need to make sure we're clear on what we're exploring and how we explore it.





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-cloth_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
class: center

.pull-left[

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_1-chair.jpg")
```

]

.pull-right[

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='95%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_1-scrooge.jpg")
```

]

???

For today you read Hoover and Donovan on "The Elements of Science."

- Let's make sure it was clear.

<br>

### What do you see on the slide?
- (A chair and a very wealthy duck)

### Based on the reading, which of these two things are concepts? 

#### - Make your case!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-cloth_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
class: center

.pull-left[

<br>

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='70%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_1-chair.jpg")
```

]

.pull-right[

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='65%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_1-scrooge.jpg")
```

]

<br>

.center[.size30[**Concepts are "names for things, feelings and ideas generated or acquired by people in the course of relating to each other and to their environment" (13).**]]

???

...

So, both of these are concepts.

- They are names for things / ideas that allow us to communicate about the world.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-scrooge2.jpg')
background-size: 100%
class: center

???

Where this gets complicated is when we disagree about the meaning of a specific concept.

### How can "wealth" be a contested concept?

<br>

#### - What are the different ways we could define "wealth" that might conflict with each other?





---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-graffiti.jpg')
background-size: 100%
class: center

???

"Wealth" is clearly a contested concept.

- To some people "wealth" is having family and friends.

- To others it is owning a fancy house and car.

- To still others it is having power to shape the world as they want it.

<br>

**The key here is to recognize that almost ALL important concepts are contested AND this is the source of MANY, if not most, fights in society.**



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_2-Types-of-Folding-Chairs.jpg')
background-size: 80%
class: center

???

### Ok, tougher test, how can a "chair" be a contested concept?

#### Not just defined differently but in ways where those definitions conflict with each other!

+ (Examples?)



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-dog_toddler.png')
background-size: 50%
class: center, slideblue

???

I know this seems silly, but clearly these two members of society define a chair differently.

### What are the stakes of this conflict?
#### - Is there anything we can learn from this photo about concepts and society?

<br>

Never forget, defining a concept is an expression of your power!

- Get others to accept your definition means you have the ability to frame our use and understanding of that thing.

This small child will clearly be impacted if we all decide to accept the dog's definition of a chair.

- And the parents were persuaded enough to stage and take the photo!

<br>

**Big point: Fights over defining concrete objects are not just silly!**




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/01-1-segregated_water_fountains.jpeg')
background-size: 100%
class: center

???

### What are we looking at here? How does this represent a political fight over defining a concept?

+ (How did we as a society define the treatment of citizens)

+ (The government policy "separate but equal" in practice)

<br>

Institutionalized racism as a government policy was very often a fight over how to define very concrete things, like water fountains, train cars and schools.

<br>

Let's bring this idea about conceptual fights to the current day.

### Give me some examples of political fights we are having in society right now that are, at their core, fights about defining a concept.

+ (SLIDE: What are the rights of the undocumented?)
+ (SLIDE: What is legitimate government assistance?)
+ (SLIDE: What is a protest?)




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-cloth_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size40[.center[**How should we define "the rights" of the undocumented?**]]

.pull-left[

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_1-No_human_being_is_illegal.jpg")
```

]

.pull-right[

```{r, fig.align='center', out.width='90%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_1-anti_immigrant.jpg")
```

]

???

What rights should the undocumented have in our society?

+ Who is entitled to protection in our society?

+ Who is a human being and who is a criminal?
    + Citizens, legal immigrants, illegal immigrants, children regardless of immigration status, asylum seekers?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-us_capitol.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: center, inverse

.textwhite[.size50[.center[**How should we define a "legitimate" government action?**]]]

???

What kinds of government action are legitimate to protect us and what are not?

+ Protection against invasion by a foreign army?

+ What if that foreign army is a disease and the protection is a vaccination?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-riot_police.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: center, inverse

.textwhite[.size50[.center[**How should we define a "legitimate" use of force?**]]]

???

I think most people would agree protection from violence is absolutely part of the definition.

### What is a legitimate use of violence by the police?

+ (When their lives are threatened?)
+ (When the lives of non-involved persons are threatened?)
+ (To protect private property?)

<br>

### What is a legitimate use of violence by protestors?

+ (None?)
+ (When their lives are threatened?)
+ (When their rights are being denied?)

<br>

### Questions on the importance and power of defining concepts?

<br>

For the purposes of this class we really can't accomplish much if we don't first define a few key concepts!



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-cloth_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[

<br>

.size35[
.textblack[**International**]

+ ?

.textblack[**Political**]

+ ?

.textblack[**Event**]

+ ?

]]

.pull-right[

```{r, fig.align='right', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_1-word_cloud.jpg")
```

]

???

All of this is why we started with the case study exercise on Monday.

Remember, our aim is not to create the perfect definition.

- That's practically impossible.

Our job is to agree on a shared definition we can use to begin exploring the world.

<br>

### Based on our case studies, what is our working definition of an "event"?

<br>

*ONLY In semesters with time in week 1 for a case study exercise*

+ Let's start with what is hopefully the easiest one, how do we define an event?



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-cloth_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[

<br>

.size35[
.textblack[**International**]

+ ?

.textblack[**Political**]

+ ?

.textblack[**Event**]

+ .textblack[A thing or happening]

]]

.pull-right[

```{r, fig.align='right', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_1-word_cloud.jpg")
```

]

???

### Again, based on our case studies, what definition of "international" did we come up with?

<br>

*ONLY in semesters with time in week 1 for a case study exercise*

+ *Ok, when you hear the word international, what do you think of?*



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-cloth_v2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle

.pull-left[

.size30[
.textblack[**International**]

+ .textblack[Global impact or]
+ .textblack[Involving > 1 state]

.textblack[**Political**]

+ ?

.textblack[**Event**]

+ .textblack[A thing or happening]

]]

.pull-right[

```{r, fig.align='right', out.width='100%'}
knitr::include_graphics("libs/Images/02_1-word_cloud.jpg")
```

]


???

Alright, now it's time for the hard one.

### Based on our case studies, what definition of "political" did we come up with?

*Discuss* and then *segue to the game*


<br>

*ONLY in semesters with time in week 1 for a case study exercise*

+ *When you think of the word "political" what do you think of?*






---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-politics_game_1.png')
background-size: 100%
class: center

???

Ok, let me see if I can help you get to how political scientists define "politics."

Let's play a game!

+ Here's the deal, it's pretty simple

+ As a class you have to distribute a couple of index cards.

<br>

## Notes on the game FOR YOU
+ Class must distribute X "Get out of jail free" cards in ten minutes

+ Each card is worth a free excused absence, a two day extension to a paper deadline (not the final) OR 1/3 letter grade bump on any paper

+ Number of cards: 3 or maybe 25% of the class?

+ **No one can just claim them by walking up and taking it. Students must make an affirmative case why they should get one**. 
    - I like this! A literal government hand out!

+ Final decisions must be reached by consensus.


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-politics_game_2.png')
background-size: 100%
class: center

???

Each of these is a "get out of jail free" card.

- You can use one of these to help yourself out of a jam in this class this semester.

- Miss a paper deadline? Add two days!

- Miss a class? Get an excused absence!

- Need to bump a paper result by 1/3 of a letter grade? Done!

<br>

Exceptions?

- Class policies, not university policies (e.g. plagiarism) or laws (e.g. no murder)

- Can't apply to the final exam

<br>

All I ask is that the class reach a consensus on the outcome.

- Once you have all agreed, let me know and I'll distribute the cards.

**That's it, that's the game. Go!**




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-2-politics1.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: right

.size40[.content-box-red[**1. What was your strategy?**]]

.size40[.content-box-red[**2. What strategies did you observe?**]]

.size40[.content-box-red[**3. What was the outcome of the game?**]]

???

In a moment I'm going to ask you to answer these three questions.
- So, take two minutes to reflect on our game and get ready to answer.

<br>

Ok, what just happened?

### 1. How did you approach this game? What was your strategy? 

### 2. What strategies did you observe from other people?

### 3. What was the outcome of the game?

### 4. Importantly, why did we end up with that outcome? Biggest reasons in your mind?

<br>

SLIDE: Assume for the moment that our game was a useful simulation of "politics"...



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-2-politics1.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

.size70[.center[.content-box-red[**Define "Politics"**]]]

???

Think about your experience and our discussion and use all of that to write me a short definition of the concept: "politics."

<br>

Alright, let's hear what you came up with!

*PRESENT and DISCUSS*

<br>

Games and simulations are often a useful tool for exploring politics.

- What we just played was a fairly basic distribution game.

- It highlights a ton of important stuff, but we'll just focus on the big idea for now.

- SLIDE: Standard definitions...


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-2-politics1.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

.size35[.right[.content-box-blue[**Decision-making in a community**]]]

<br>

.size35[.right[.content-box-blue[**How we make and enforce "the rules"**]]]

<br>

.size35[.right[.content-box-blue[**Who gets what, when and how?**]]]

???

In various textbooks you'll see politics defined as:

1. Decision-making in a community
    - One important aspect of "politics" is the study of how groups make decisions.
    - Group decision-making is complex and it multilayered
    - It is also one of the fundamental building blocks of our society 
    
2. How we make and enforce "the rules"
    - Once a society has established rules, many political scientists study how those rules are interpreted, revised and enforced
    - The decision to punish a rule-breaker is a huge decision that often sends ripples out through a community

### Out of curiosity, what would happen if today SGF police started pulling over EVERY car that exceeded the speed limit by even 1 mph?

<br>

### And what would happen if they completely stopped enforcing speed limits?




---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02-2-politics1.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center

.size35[.right[.content-box-blue[**Decision-making in a community**]]]

<br>

.size35[.right[.content-box-blue[**How we make and enforce "the rules"**]]]

<br>

.size35[.right[.content-box-blue[**Who gets what, when and how?**]]]

???

3) Who gets what, when and how?
- Why do groups form? Often it is to produce greater resources than any individual could do on their own
- This could be money, food, a division of labor, or collective security
- HOWEVER, producing that good means someone has to pay and someone has to benefit
- Who pays and who benefits is a constant tension in any society.

<br>

Bottom line, anytime a group of people have to come together to make a decision, a political scientist can be found who is interested in the process.

### Any questions on this broad definition of the concept "politics"?

<br>

Now let's talk about the "science" part of our reading for today.

- Hoover and Donovan do a really nice job laying out how social scientists can follow a process in order to generate useful knowledge about the world.

- For your participation point today I asked each of you to answer a fairly important question for me...



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-monkey_darts_politics.jpg')
background-size: 75%
class: center

???

### Based on the reading, what do you think is the biggest challenge to doing political science as a "science"?

*Give them time to explain and explore. Call on people if you must!*

<br>

### Can social scientists follow the scientific method? Why or why not?
+ ("The point is that the scientific method seeks to test thoughts against observable evidence in a disciplined manner, with each step in the process made explicit" (H&D old p24))

(p29: One version of the scientific method)
1. Identify the concepts / variables to be studied
2. Form a hypothesis (...proposes a relationship between two or more variables p26)
3. Gather evidence
4. Compare evidence to hypotheses, derive conclusions
5. Discuss the uncertainty in those conclusions


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-science_process_meme.png')
background-size: 50%
class: center, slideblue

???

The important takeaway for us.

Science is a process for generating knowledge, not a bucket of facts about the natural world.

- Science is NEVER just about taking someone’s word for something.

- Knowledge that cannot be independently confirmed is not useful.

- We rely only on “observable evidence”

- “each step...made explicit” (p24)

<br>

You can study anything "scientifically."

- Even politics!

### Make sense?






---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-theory_defined.png')
background-size: 100%
class: center

???

I also want to introduce the idea of theory today.

"...a set of related propositions that suggest why events occur in the manner that they do" (p32).

### Any famous theories come to mind?
+ (Relativity?)
+ (Gravity?)
+ (The big bang?)

<br>

In simple terms, theories are the arguments we use to explain why things happen.

We'll get more into this on Friday.

The bottom-line at this point is that thinking theoretically makes you a much smarter and more capable citizen.



---

background-image: url('libs/Images/02_1-politics_game_2.png')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: center

???

Let's end today by applying a scientific perspective to our distribution game.

<br>

Imagine I'm about to bring you to a place where you can observe a different class play the distribution game with the same rules I gave you today.

### Prediction Time! If I asked you to predict who in that class would end up with the cards, what information would you want about the students? Why?

*ON BOARD*

<br>

### BRAINSTORM: Propose a rule change to the game and tell me how it would impact the outcome

*ON BOARD*

- (Gender breakdown?)
- (% Student athletes?)
- (Extroverts vs introverts?)
- (Class size?)
- (When during the semester is it?)

<br>

All of your ideas here on the board represent EITHER:

1. A reflection of the theories/models in your head that guide your assessment of how politics works, OR

2. the building blocks of new political science theories in the making.

I think that's super cool.


---

background-image: url('libs/Images/background-blue_triangles.jpg')
background-size: 100%
background-position: center
class: middle, center

.size70[.content-box-purple[**Assignment for Next Class**]

Mingst & Arreguin-Toft (2017) "Chapter 3, International Relations Theories"
]

???

For Friday you'll be reading a chapter that will introduce you to some of the big, grand-daddy theories of International Relations.

### Questions on anything from today?
